chunk_summary: This section outlines the triage and first-line management of heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) in adolescents, emphasizing the prevention of iron deficiency anemia and the identification of bleeding disorders through history, examination, and targeted investigations.
learning_objectives: Understand the triage process for adolescents with heavy menstrual bleeding, including assessment of hemodynamic stability.
Identify key historical and clinical signs of bleeding disorders in adolescents with HMB.
Learn first-line medical management options for HMB, including tranexamic acid and NSAIDs.
Recognize the importance of treating iron deficiency anemia and when to refer to specialists.
key_facts: HMB in adolescents can lead to severe iron deficiency anemia and may indicate underlying bleeding disorders.
Triage includes assessing hemodynamic stability using pulse, blood pressure, and orthostatic vitals.
Minimum investigations include CBC with indices, ferritin, pregnancy testing (if indicated), and targeted coagulation studies.
Tranexamic acid is dosed at 15–25 mg/kg (max 1 g) 2–3 times daily on heavy days.
NSAIDs can be used during menses unless contraindicated, and iron deficiency should be treated to restore hemoglobin and replenish stores.
Pelvic examination is not mandatory for all adolescents and should be individualized based on symptoms and safeguarding context.
reference_quote: Heavy menstrual bleeding: Assessment and management (NICE NG88) — Control bleeding and correct anemia in parallel.
document_key_topics: Triage for heavy menstrual bleeding in adolescents
Assessment of bleeding disorders and anemia
Role of investigations and specialist referral
